skill.name: writing-hookify-rules
skill.description: Guides users through creating local Hookify rules that watch Bash commands, file edits, stop events, and prompts using markdown frontmatter and regex patterns.
skill.summary: Create Hookify rule files with events, patterns, conditions, and clear warning or blocking messages.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many dangerous command, code execution, and secret-related strings in SKILL.md. Human review found these are markdown examples for writing defensive Hookify rules, not executable code, command execution, or credential access. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, network behavior, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Terms Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The eval and exec terms appear inside a regex example and a one-line Python regex test command. They are presented as patterns users may detect, not as skill logic that evaluates untrusted code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section is Pattern Writing Tips and Testing Patterns. The text explains regex matching and does not define executable skill code.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Shell Commands Are Defensive Rule Patterns
low_findings.description: The rm, dd, mkfs, sudo, and chmod strings are listed as examples of commands that Hookify rules can detect or block. They are not instructions for the skill to run those commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are under bash event guidance and common pattern advice. The content tells users how to detect risky commands rather than execute them.

low_findings.title: Secret File Terms Are Defensive Matching Examples
low_findings.description: The .env, API_KEY, credentials, and .pem references are examples for warning about sensitive files or secret-like text. No file read, environment variable access, or network exfiltration behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited strings appear in rule examples, common pattern lists, or example filenames. No evidence found of actual secret collection or access.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent guardrails for risky commands, sensitive edits, and completion checks. This skill helps create local Hookify rules with clear events, patterns, conditions, and messages.
actual_capabilities: Explains the markdown and YAML frontmatter format for Hookify rules.
Shows how to choose bash, file, stop, prompt, or all events.
Documents simple regex patterns and multi-condition rule formats.
Provides examples for warnings, blocking actions, and completion reminders.
Gives naming, storage, testing, disabling, and refinement guidance.
limitations: Does not install Hookify or verify that the runtime is configured.
Does not execute or validate rules against a live project by itself.
Regex examples may need adjustment for each repository and shell style.
Local rules can be too broad if users do not test them carefully.

use_cases.title: Add Command Guardrails
use_cases.description: Create rules that warn about or block risky shell commands before an AI agent runs them.

use_cases.title: Protect Sensitive Files
use_cases.description: Build file edit rules that warn when changes touch local secrets, credentials, or generated output.

use_cases.title: Standardize Completion Checks
use_cases.description: Define stop-event reminders for tests, builds, documentation, or release steps before work is considered complete.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Rule
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one risky command pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify rule that warns when a Bash command includes a risky delete pattern. Use a simple pattern and a clear message.

prompt_templates.title: Add a File Edit Warning
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Hookify file rule that warns when an edit touches an .env file and the new text includes an API key.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Condition Rule
prompt_templates.scenario: Match a specific file type and a specific code pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify rule with conditions for TypeScript files that warns when new text includes console logging.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Team Rule Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow guardrails for a repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design four Hookify rules for this repository: one bash rule, one file rule, one stop checklist, and one prompt rule. Include names, events, actions, patterns, and messages.

output_examples.input: User asks for a rule that warns about deleting files recursively.
output_examples.output: A complete Hookify rule description with a descriptive name, bash event, delete-command pattern, warning action, and a safety-focused message.

output_examples.input: User asks for protection around local secret files.
output_examples.output: A file-event rule plan that checks the file path and new text, then reminds the agent to avoid committing sensitive values.

output_examples.input: User asks for a completion checklist before stopping.
output_examples.output: A stop-event rule that reminds the agent to confirm tests, build status, and documentation updates before ending the task.

best_practices: Use narrow patterns and conditions so rules trigger only on relevant behavior.
Write messages that explain the risk and give a practical next step.
Test each rule immediately and refine broad matches before relying on it.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague rule names that hide the purpose of the guardrail.
Do not create broad patterns that match common words across unrelated files.
Do not use blocking actions until the warning version has been tested.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It helps create markdown Hookify rule files with YAML frontmatter, matching patterns, optional conditions, and user-facing messages.

faq.question: Where are Hookify rules stored?
faq.answer: The skill documents local rule files stored under the .claude directory with hookify-specific local filenames.

faq.question: Can it block commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains the block action for rules, but users should test warning rules first.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation for writing rule files. Command strings in the file are examples to detect.

faq.question: Does it support multiple conditions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains condition fields, operators, and matching rules that require all conditions to pass.

faq.question: Is regex knowledge required?
faq.answer: Basic regex knowledge helps. The skill includes escaping tips, examples, and common pattern pitfalls.
